sanskrit_devanagari:
तस्मादसक्तः सततं कार्यं कर्म समाचर ।
असक्तो ह्याचरन्कर्म परमाप्नोति पूरुषः ॥ १९ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
tasmād asaktaḥ satataṁ
kāryaṁ karma samācara |
asakto hy ācaran karma
param āpnoti pūruṣaḥ || 19 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence as published)
exact (text body identical with vedabase; punctuation rendering differs)
exact (text body identical; punctuation rendering differs)
exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_03.019 = MBh_06,025.019). Body text identical; word-segmentation differs by edition.

translations.verbatim_quote: Therefore, without being attached to the fruits of activities, one should act as a matter of duty, for by working without attachment one attains the Supreme.
(See holy-bhagavad-gita.org for full Mukundananda rendering; agreement with Prabhupada noted in disagreements section where it diverges materially.)

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The Supreme is the Personality of Godhead for the devotees, and liberation for the impersonalist. A person, therefore, acting for Kṛṣṇa, or in Kṛṣṇa consciousness, under proper guidance and without attachment to the result of the work, is certainly making progress toward the supreme goal of life.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.19 Since this is so, therefore, asaktah, remaining unattached; samacara, perform; satatam, always; karyam, the obligatory; daily karma, duty; hi, for; acaran, by performing; (one’s) karma, duty; asaktah, without attachment, by doing work as a dedication to God; purusah, a person
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara: tasmāt — therefore — having distinguished the realised one (3.17-3.18), the rest of you, asaktaḥ (without attachment), satatam (continuously), kāryaṁ karma samācara (perform the action that ought to be done). The unattached actor reaches param — the Supreme — which Shankara reads as either liberation directly (for the qualified aspirant) or as cittaśuddhi (purification of mind) leading to it.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.19 Therefore, considering that work has to be performed with detachment, you perform it, considering yourself a non-agent. This will be declared in the words ‘with detachment’ and ‘which ought to be done,’ meaning that one attains the Supreme by Karma Yoga itself.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja: for the aspirant established in sattva (rather than the realised one of 3.17-3.18), the prescription is asaktaḥ — without attachment to the fruit — perform the action that ought to be done. 'Param āpnoti' here is the Lord — karma-yoga performed without attachment, with the Lord as the orientation, leads to the Lord.

positions.rendering: without attachment, performing one's duty... attains the Supreme
without attachment, perform the duty enjoined upon you... attains the Supreme
the unattached actor attains parama (Brahman, mokṣa); karma-yoga is here equated with jñāna-yoga in result
the unattached actor attains the Lord; karma-yoga performed for the Lord is the path to mokṣa for the aspirant in sattva

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The traditions agree on the path (asaktaḥ — without attachment) but disagree on the destination. Shankara reads param as Brahman / liberation; for him, karma-yoga is preparatory to or sometimes equivalent in result with jñāna-yoga (depending on the aspirant's state). Ramanuja reads param as the Lord; for him, karma-yoga IS the path for the embodied actor, not merely preparatory. Translators flatten both into 'the Supreme'.

literal_meaning: Therefore, always perform action that ought to be done, without attachment; for the man who acts without attachment attains the Supreme.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The pivot. After naming the exception (3.17-3.18), Krishna returns to the rule for the actor still in the field of action: perform kāryam karma (the act that ought to be done) asaktaḥ (without attachment), satatam (always, continuously). The promise is paramāpnoti — attains the Supreme — placing karma-yoga (acting without attachment) on the same liberative plane as jñāna-yoga.
